Srrreturg rf Stutp
1230 J STREET
S^CR^MENro, C^LIFoRNT^ 958 l4
December 6, 19 91
Scott HaIIabrin, Esqui re
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 .j Street, Su j.te iJ00
Sacramento, Ca I ifornia 95814
Re: The March Fong Eu Foundation
Dear Mr
Hallabrin:
The l{arch Eong Eu Foundation was incorporated on June 6,
1985, as a California nonprofit public benefit corporation
for charitable purposes under the California Nonprofit
Public Benefit Corporation taw, As set forth in its
articles, the specific purpose of the foundation is to
provide grants to students to further their education. It
was assigned Corporate Number 1277371 by the Office of the
California Secretary of State. On July 25, 1986, the
Franchise Tax Board of California issued an exemption
letter evidencing the foundation's California tax exempt
status pursuant to Revenue and Taxation Code section
28701(d). On September 25, L986, the foundation filed
Form I023 (Application for Recognition of Exemption) with
the Internal Revenue Service as a tax exempt "private"
foundation. The application was denied, without
prejudice, pending submission of specific operating
procedures. March Fong Eu, as the incorporator, has
appointed interim directors. 
No meetings of the boarcl of
directors have been held.
The foundation has remained "inactive" although all
appropriate documents have been filed with the taxing and
other enforcement agencies. No funds have been received
or spent by the foundation. The directors have indicated
an interest in activating the foundation and application
is expected to be made in the near fuLure for federal tax
exempt ion.


Scott HaIlabrin
December 6, 19 91
Page 2
Although detailed operating procedures are stiIl 
in the
process of being formulated, it is expected that March
Fong Eu wilI be personally involvecl in the management of
the foundation and the solicitation of funds ancl
authorizing expenditures for charitable purposes, It is
possible that Dr. Eu may contribute personal artwork and
other personal items to the foundation which woulil be useal
for fundraising activities. 
The foundation wilL comply
ful1y with aII provisions of tax and other laws.
March Fong Eu is, of course, Secretary of State of
California. 
She has filed Form 501 declaring her intent
to seek re-election in L994 and has formed the March Fong
Eu Campaign Committee 1994 to promote her candidacy at
said election.
on behalf of March Fong Eu, I request informal advice as
to what reporting obligations and what restrictions appIy,
if any, under the PoliticaI Reform Act, with respect to
the outlined activities of, and Dr. Eu's association with,
The March Fong Eu Foundation. Specifically, need
contributions to and expenditur'es of the foundation be
reportecl on campaign reports or statements of economic
interest? would contributions to the foundation be
subject to the "gift" Iimits imposed on elected
officials? 
Are there any other concerns which might arise
with respect to foundation activities, 
as outlined above,
about which March Fong Eu should be advised in terms of
the PoliticaI Reform Act?
Your assistance in this regard is very much appreciated.
MI LLER
S i ncere

sections.question: On behalf of March Fong Eu, I request informal advice as to what reporting obligations and what restrictions apply, if any, under the Political Reform Act, with respect to the outlined activities of, and Dr. Eu's association with, The March Fong Eu Foundation. Specifically, need contributions to and expenditures of the foundation be reported on campaign reports or statements of economic interest? Would contributions to the foundation be subject to the "gift" limits imposed on elected officials? Are there any other concerns which might arise with respect to foundation activities, as outlined above, about which March Fong Eu should be advised in terms of the Political Reform Act?
sections.question_synthetic: The inquiry seeks FPPC advice on whether contributions to and expenditures of the March Fong Eu Foundation must be reported on campaign reports or statements of economic interest, whether contributions are subject to gift limits for elected officials, and what other Political Reform Act concerns may apply to Secretary of State March Fong Eu's involvement with the foundation.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to FPPC, not an FPPC response letter. The document contains a detailed question section but no conclusion or advice from FPPC. Date is December 6, 1991, addressed to Scott Hallabrin at FPPC's Legal Division.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: On behalf of March Fong Eu, I request informal advice as to what reporting obligations and what restrictions apply, if any, under the Political Reform Act, with respect to the outlined activities of, and Dr. Eu's association with, The March Fong Eu Foundation. Specifically, need contributions to and expenditures of the foundation be reported on campaign reports or statements of economic interest? Would contributions to the foundation be subject to the "gift" limits imposed on elected officials? Are there any other concerns which might arise with respect to foundation activities, as outlined above, about which March Fong Eu should be advised in terms of the Political Reform Act?

embedding.summary: This is a request for informal FPPC advice from the Secretary of State's office regarding reporting obligations and Political Reform Act restrictions applicable to Secretary of State March Fong Eu's involvement with the March Fong Eu Foundation, a nonprofit charitable foundation providing educational grants to students.
